Painting: lost; four soldiers fight for a standard

front: Painting: in the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan; commissioned by Ludovico Sforza
Painting: all thirteen figures on the same side of the table; Judas knocks over a salt cellar; Judas's face in darkness
Painting: used oil/tempera on plaster (not true fresco) — began decaying immediately; Vasari called it a 'muddle of blots'
Painting: used Jesus's cheek as the vanishing point
Painting: Andy Warhol's last artwork was sixty silkscreen prints of this scene
Painting: described by Henry James as the 'saddest work of art in the world'; 15-minute viewing limit due to its condition
Painting: portrait of Jesus holding a crystal orb; raises two fingers; sold for $450M — most expensive painting ever
Painting: controversially attributed to Leonardo; Walter Isaacson argued the orb is rendered unrealistically; Martin Kemp defends attribution
Painting: Marcel Duchamp added a mustache and goatee to a postcard of it, titled L.H.O.O.Q.
Painting: stolen in 1911 by Vincenzo Peruggia; Guillaume Apollinaire implicated Pablo Picasso
Painting: Fernando Botero parodied the subject at 'Age Twelve'
Painting: scans by Pascal Cotte showed the subject originally had visible eyebrows
Artist: notebooks over 7,000 pages in left-handed mirror script; Codex Leicester owned by Bill Gates since 1994
Artist: used as model for Plato in Raphael's School of Athens (holds Timaeus, points finger upward)
Artist: the only painting by this artist publicly viewable in the Americas is his portrait of Ginevra de' Benci (National Gallery, Washington)
Artist: Ingres painted this man's deathbed being attended by King Francis I of France
Artist: student of Verrocchio; modeled as the Archangel Raphael in Verrocchio's Tobias and the Angel
Artist: teacher of Salai (nickname 'little devil'); Salai modeled for John the Baptist and the homoerotic Angel in the Flesh drawing
Artist: horse sculpture for Ludovico Sforza abandoned when 70 tons of bronze were diverted to cast cannons
Artist: earliest known commissioned painting was the Annunciation; figure holds a white lily representing Florence
Artist: grotesque head drawings; the Ugly Duchess by Quentin Matsys may derive from a lost Leonardo grotesque head
Painting: London altarpiece version flanked by a Bernardino Luini green angel and an Ambrogio de Predis red angel with lute
back: Virgin of the Rocks (Leonardo da Vinci)

front: Painting: earliest description is sonnet by Bernardo Bellincioni: 'Why are you angry? Whom do you envy, nature?'
back: Lady with an Ermine (Leonardo da Vinci)

front: Painting: 20th century restoration removed footprint of an SS soldier; background repainted black in 18th century, making it resemble La Belle Ferronnière
back: Lady with an Ermine (Leonardo da Vinci)

front: Painting: earliest known photograph posted on a Baton Rouge real estate agency's website
Painting: Yves Bouvier was arrested for dealing it to Dmitry Rybolovlev
